reasoning: The exploitation prerequisite chain requires clearing three sequential gates, all of which are restricted to WordPress Administrator-level users in a default install. Gate 1 (line 58): `current_user_can('edit_theme_options')` — requires at minimum an Editor role. Gate 2 (line 44, `can_import()`): `current_user_can('install_plugins')` — this is an Administrator-only capability in WordPress default role configuration; Editors and below do not have it. Gate 3 (line 251): `wp_verify_nonce($_REQUEST['cei-import'], 'cei-importing')` — the import nonce is issued only to users who can load the Customizer admin UI, meaning the gate value is never exposed to unauthenticated or low-privilege users. The template check at line 308 is bypassable by an Administrator who already has export access, but this is moot given the capability requirement. An unauthenticated attacker cannot obtain any of these gate values. A low-privilege attacker (Subscriber, Contributor, Author, Editor) is blocked at Gate 2. Only an Administrator can reach the vulnerable `do_action('customize_save_' . $key)` sink at line 345. In a WordPress multi-admin scenario this represents an admin-to-admin escalation of unintended hook dispatch, which is not meaningfully exploitable beyond what an admin can already do. Severity is downgraded from MEDIUM to LOW.
